Foam

Probability Distributions

Discrete Distributions

Bernoulli Trial

There are only two possible outcomes - success and failure.

\[P(X) = (1-\theta) \text{ if x = 0 else }\theta\]

Binomial Distribution

When we have nnn Bernoulli trials, we end up with a binomial distribution. Our random variable XXX here represents the number of successes in these nnn Bernoulli trials.

We therefore have a pdf defined as

\[P(x=r) = {n \choose r} \theta^r (1-\theta)^{n-r}\]

where rrr represents the number of successes.

As nnn tends to infinity, we find that our binomial distribution in turn tends to a normal distribution.

Negative Binomial

XXX here represents the number of bernoulli trials to be observed till a fixed number of successes is observed. θ\thetaθ here represents the probability of success. This gives us the pdf of

\[P(X=r) = {r-1 \choose k-1} \theta^k(1-\theta)^{r-k}\]

The intuition here is that the final trial alwaus involves the k−thk-thk−th success. Therefore we permute the initial r−1r-1r−1 trials which involves k−1k-1k−1 successes.

The sum of independent negative binomials having the same value of θ\thetaθ is also interestingly negative binomial.

The expectaiton of this distribution is kθ\frac{k}{\theta}θk​ and the variance is k(1−θ)θ2\frac{k(1-\theta)}{\theta^2}θ2k(1−θ)​

Geometric

The geometric distribution is concerned with the number of trials till the first success. We therefore have a pdf of

\[P(x=k) = (1-\theta)^{k-1}\theta\]

The expectation of this distribution is 1θ\frac{1}{\theta}θ1​ and its variance is 1−θθ2\frac{1-\theta}{\theta^2}θ21−θ​

Poisson

The poisson distribution measures the number of events observed in a fixed time period given a rate of occurence.

We have a probability density function of

\[P(x=r) = \frac{\theta^re^{-\theta}}{r!}\]

Interestingly, the mean and the variance of the poisson is its rate paramter. In this case, that is θ\thetaθ

Hypergeometric

The hypergeometric distribution shows the probability of obtaining rrr succeses in a population of size NNN when drawing a sample of size nnn. In this population, we have RRR successes and N−RN-RN−R failures.

We can calculate the probability of obtaining rrr successes by taking

\[p(x=r) = \frac { {R \choose r} {N-R \choose n-r} } { {N \choose n} }\]

Continous Distributions

Gamma

The gamma distribution measures the time until k−thk-thk−th subsequent events occur. It has the density function of

\[p(x;\lambda,\alpha) = \frac{\lambda^{\alpha}x^{\alpha}e^{-\lambda x}}{\Gamma(x)}\]

The gamma is often used to model non-negative values and is simply the result of kkk individual exponential distributions. Another way to think about this is that the exponential is a gamma distribution with a rate parameter λ\lambdaλ of 1.

It has an expected value of αλ\frac{\alpha}{\lambda}λα​ and a variance of αλ2\frac{\alpha}{\lambda^2}λ2α​.

Exponential

The exponential distribution measures the time till the first occurence of an event. Since Γ(1)=1\Gamma(1) = 1Γ(1)=1, we can rewrite our gamma function pdf to give us the pdf of the exponential distribution as

\[P(x;\theta) = \theta e^{-\theta x}\]

It has an expected value of 1θ\frac{1}{\theta}θ1​ and a variance of 1θ2\frac{1}{\theta^2}θ21​

Normal Distribution

The Normal distribution has the pdf of

\[P(x;\mu , \sigma^2 ) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}exp(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2})\]

Chi-Squared

The Chi-Squared distribution is defined as the squared-sum of nnn samples drawn from a standard normal distribution. The degrees of freedom of a chi-squared distribution is equal to the number of samples involved.
